title: Recreating the succulent garden
body: I got this big, shallow yellow pot a couple of years ago for a succulent garden. Most of its inhabitants have since died, so I decided it was time for a full refresh. I set a couple of big rocks over the drainage hole at the bottom to keep the soil from clogging it and to encourage draining, then filled it with a soil mix of equal parts cactus dirt, grit, and sand. And then I planted it up!

Two of its new inhabitants are fresh acquisitions from McGuckin — a variegated Euphorbia neriifolia and a spear-head Senecio (both pictured individually here) — plus the one lone survivor from the last garden, plus a bunch of cuttings from elsewhere that I'm hoping will root in. (There's a little green dinosaur in there too, obviously.)

While I was at it, I also repotted a couple of other little ones out of the small 2-inch pots they came in from the farmers market plant haul and into small ceramics, and took a cutting off each for the new garden.

Oh, and there's a new silly little onion plant from McGuckin too. I was going to put it in the garden but held off for now — I might tuck it in later when I divide it, but I'm still figuring out what to do with it.

shots.cap: The refreshed garden, planted up in the shallow yellow pot: new acquisitions, one survivor, and a scatter of hopeful cuttings — dinosaur included.
New from McGuckin: a variegated Euphorbia neriifolia, cream-edged paddles on a spiny stem.
The other McGuckin pickup — a spear-head Senecio (Senecio kleiniiformis), all blue-green arrowhead leaves.
One of the little farmers-market plants, repotted from its 2-inch nursery pot into a small terracotta and top-dressed with river rock.
The other repotted little one — a dusky, bronze-rose rosette settling into fresh gritty mix.
The silly little onion plant (Ornithogalum caudatum), sitting out the garden for now.
